result:
I.  
The first thing to notice is the hum, a low, almost sub-audible thrum that seems to come from the floorboards themselves. It is not the refrigerator, though the refrigerator also hums; it is not the traffic on the interstate three miles away, though on certain nights when the wind aligns you can hear the eighteen-wheelers change gears. This hum is older than those sounds. It is older than the house, older than the town platted in 1837 along a bend in the river that used to catch the barges and swing them slow as dancers. If you press your bare feet to the pine boards at 3:12 a.m.—I checked the stove clock once, bleary-eyed, because numbers feel like evidence—you can feel the hum travel up through the bones of the legs, past the hinge of the knees, along the femoral artery until it reaches the small drum of the heart, which answers in its own accent.  

Some nights I stand on the floor and imagine the hum as a long dark thread let down from somewhere above the roof, a thread that wants to reel me in like a hooked fish. Other nights I imagine it as a seam: if I could find the right blade I might split the world open like a ripe melon and discover what is really underneath. I have never told anyone this. I have never told anyone a lot of things, because language is a sieve and most of what matters drips straight through.

II.  
When I was eight I believed the house knew my thoughts. Not in a magical-thinking way—no, I was a pragmatic child, the kind who arranged her stuffed rabbits according to strict fiscal hierarchies: who had vested interests, who held majority shares, who might stage a coup. I believed the house possessed a form of sentience made of lathe and horsehair plaster, that it registered every pulse of longing or shame and filed it away inside the walls like extra insulation. I tested the theory once by standing in the upstairs hallway and thinking, as loudly as I could, I hate you. I waited for a reply. What I got was the bathroom door drifting open on its tarnished hinges, a slow exhale that sounded almost consoling. After that I tried to think only neutral, factual things—The capital of Honduras is Tegucigalpa—but of course the damage was done. The house knew. The hum began shortly after, or maybe it had always been there and I had only grown ears.

III.  
There is a moment, when you have been driving for so many hours that the white lines begin to braid themselves into DNA helices, when you realize the body is just a container of smaller containers, an infinitely collapsible set of Russian dolls. Inside the torso the heart inside its sac; inside the heart the four chambers; inside each chamber a microscopic teacup of blood; inside the teacup platelets shaped like jellyfish; inside the platelets the proteins that remember every ancestor who outran the wolf or did not. The highway at night is an exercise in relinquishing scale: you are simultaneously the semitrailer booming past and the bug sizzling against its headlamp. Somewhere near the state line I became convinced that if I flicked the high beams just right I would illuminate the corridor where the generations queue, waiting their turn to climb into the cab and grab the wheel. I didn’t dare try; instead I pulled off at a Shell station where the fluorescent lights turned my skin the color of mortuary clay and bought a cup of coffee so acid it could have etched glass. On the styrofoam cup somebody had written in black marker: WE SEE YOU. I still have the cup. It sits on a shelf in the kitchen like a relic, the letters flaking a little more each year, because I cannot decide whether it was a promise or a threat.

IV.  
Once, in the fluorescent-lit labyrinth of a hospital corridor, a neurologist told me that memory is not a file cabinet but a story we agree to keep retelling. Each time we open the file—or tell the story—the brain rewrites it slightly, like a photocopy of a photocopy until the original image is gone and what remains is the ghost of the ghost. I asked him whether that means the past is essentially fictional. He shrugged, which is what scientists do when they have run out of grant money to investigate metaphysics. I left clutching a sheaf of papers that advised me to “externalize memory” through journaling, photography, voice memos. So I bought a notebook. I took pictures of the front porch, the mailbox, the neighbor’s one-eyed tabby who sunbathes on the hood of my car. Six months later the notebook was nowhere to be found; the photos had corrupted themselves into mosaics of emerald and fuchsia, as though the pixels had decided to party while my back was turned. The only thing left was the hum, unchanged, persistent, like a narrator who refuses to be edited out.

V.  
The river, the one the town was built around, used to be wider. You can see the scars on the bluffs where the water slithered up in flood years and carried off whole orchards. My great-grandfather planted a pear orchard on the bottomland, the kind of pears you have to wait until the first frost to pick, when the cells begin to rupture and the flesh converts panic into sugar. The trees survived the ’37 flood by clinging together, roots interlaced like desperate fingers. After the water receded they bore fruit the size of balled-up fists, hard and sweet, and the family made them into jars of butter that lasted through the Depression. My mother remembers the taste as “brown sunlight.” The orchard is gone now—subdivisions clipped onto the loam with names like Heron’s Landing though no heron would be caught dead beside a retention pond shaped like an amoeba. Still, every spring a few rogue saplings sprout along the old levee, and the city maintenance crews mow them down, and every spring they rise again, stubborn as heresy. Sometimes I park the car across from the levee and watch for the first green dagger of leaf. I never wait to see if the crews will come. I don’t want the witness; I want the persistence.

VI.  
I have a recurring dream in which I am trying to mail a letter but cannot find the correct denomination of stamps. The envelope grows heavier the longer I hold it, until it drags on the ground like a lead apron. I open it to check the address and inside is a miniature house, my house, complete with the hum. Tiny me stands in the attic, waving frantically. I try to lick the stamp but my tongue turns to sand. When I wake the pillow is wet—whether from tears or saliva I never know. I tell myself dreams are just neural flatulence, but the next day I always find myself at the post office squinting at the new stamp designs: tulip trees, astronaut cats, muscle cars, social-justice pioneers. None of them seem adequate postage for whatever I am trying to send. I leave empty-handed, buy a coffee, stare at the cup, remember the WE SEE YOU, remember that I am seen, remember that I do not know by whom, remember that the house is still humming.

VII.  
There is comfort in small routines: feeding the sourdough starter every morning, sniffing to make sure it smells of green apples and not acetone; rotating the car tires so they wear evenly, a modest rebellion against impermanence; alphabetizing the spice rack while the pasta water comes to a boil, running my thumb along the jars of peppercorns, sumac, za’atar, as though they were runes. Marjoram for forgetting, fenugreek for courage, nigella for the parts of the soul that leak out in sleep. I read once that medieval monks kept miniature gardens in wooden boxes so they could pray while traveling—each herb a mnemonic for a psalm. I wonder if they ever doubted the efficacy, ever stood in a flea-bitten inn courtyard and thought, What if none of this counts? What if the world is just a series of random cruelties and my thyme dies with me? I like to think they kept tending the box anyway, because the knuckles must do something while the heart is on hold.

VIII.  
I used to believe there was a correct order to biography: birth, education, love, work, decline, death, like beads on a rosary. Then I met a woman who collects abandoned grocery lists. She has hundreds, dating back to the 1970s, slipped into plastic sleeves and filed in three-ring binders. One reads simply: bananas, rat poison, birthday card for Mom. Another: gluten-free flour, lube, Valium, maybe cilantro? She told me these fragments are more honest than any autobiography because nobody expects to be preserved. I asked if she had a favorite. She pulled out a list written on the back of a parking ticket: milk, milk, milk, milk, milk. “That one,” she said, “is a poem.” I keep thinking about the person who needed that much milk, who crossed the same word five times as though repetition could conjure calcium, as though calcium could conjure safety. Did they drink it all? Did they pour it down the sink and return to buy more, caught in a Möbius strip of craving and purging? Did they ever notice the ticket on the windshield, did they crumple both together and stuff them in a pocket, did the paper go through the wash and emerge a compressed lint coin, did the washer flood, did the basement fill, did the hum get louder? I asked if I could photocopy the list. She said no, the fragility is the point. I left clutching nothing but the afterimage of blue ink on glossy paper, five exes marking a spot.

IX.  
In the attic I keep a box of obsolete chargers: Nokia bricks, flip-phone umbilicals, a tangle of coaxial cables thick as pythons. They are relics of a future that arrived too soon and departed without them, like children crowned kings and then exiled. Sometimes I spread them on the floor and try to guess which once carried my voice to my mother’s ear during ice storms, which ferried grainy photos of newborns, which transmitted break-up texts in all-caps. The copper inside is mined from places I will never see, by hands I will never shake, under a sun that burned them darker than the mine owners’ consciences. I picture the cables as blood vessels in a planetary body, severed and coagulating. I picture them as narratives: the braided tale of how badly we wanted connection, how eagerly we soldered and sheathed and sold it, how quickly we left the husks behind. When the box gets too full I sneak it into the electronics bin at the dump, but somehow, months later, it respawns in the attic like a Hydra head. I have stopped wondering where they come from; I have started wondering where I go.

X.  
The neighbors acquired a device that repels mosquitoes by emitting a frequency only the insects can hear. It is called, with depressing literalism, the Mosquito. They mounted it on the back porch, aimed at the hedge where the fireflies conduct their silent orgies. Within a week I noticed the absence: no tap-tap of June bugs on the screen, no mosquito hawks helicoptering in the dusk. The air had been vacuumed of its smallest percussion. I sat on my own porch and felt the silence settle into the joint of my jaw like ice. I wanted to tell them that removing one note collapses the chord, that the ecosystem is a house of cards wearing a house of cards as a hat. Instead I Googled “how to hack Mosquito device” and learned it oscillates at 17.4 kHz, just above the upper range of human teenagers. I downloaded an app that plays the opposite waveform, inverted 180 degrees, supposedly canceling the silence. I propped my phone on the railing and let it whine all night. The cat yowled; the neighbors complained; I gained no mosquitoes back. What returned instead was the hum, louder than ever, as though it had absorbed the missing wings and learned their frequencies. I apologized to the cat, unplugged the app, accepted that some forms of repair only widen the wound.

XI.  
I once read about a Japanese practice called kintsugi: mending broken pottery with lacquer mixed with powdered gold, highlighting the cracks instead of hiding them. The philosophy is that damage is a history to be honored. I wonder what becomes of a vessel too shattered to hold liquid, too fragmented for even gold to embrace. Do you grind it to dust and scatter it in the garden? Do you press the dust into pills and swallow a memory each morning with your antidepressants? Or do you dump it in a shoebox and shove the box under the bed, where the monsters have unionized and now hold committee meetings about lighting and dental. I tried kintsugi on an old coffee mug that had witnessed every breakup and all-nighter of my twenties. The epoxy smelled like despair and refused to set; the gold powder clumped like sarcasm. The mug now leaks along its entire circumference, a golden drool that puddles on the table like a small, shameful sun. I use it anyway. Each morning I pour coffee and watch it weep, thinking: this is what becomes of healing when vanity insists on starring in the role.

XII.  
Time dilates in laundromats. The spin cycle becomes a cosmology: the drum a galaxy, the socks rogue planets, the lint trap a black hole archiving the DNA of every garment you ever loved. I once lost a sock embroidered with tiny yellow giraffes. I waited through three full cycles, convinced it would reappear, a cotton Lazarus. It never did. Weeks later I found myself at the grocery store staring at a woman wearing mismatched socks: one solid navy, one populated by miniature giraffes. I followed her down the aisle of canned beans, heart jack-hammering, rehearsing polite ways to accuse a stranger of sock theft. At the self-checkout she lifted her foot to scratch an ankle and I saw the price tag still dangling: new socks. Pure coincidence. I went home and emptied my own sock drawer like a detective overturning a suspect’s mattress. Nothing. The sock is somewhere, perhaps in the lint-trap singularity, perhaps in a parallel hamper where the colors run in reverse and all the lost left socks form a union with all the lost right Tupperware lids. I like to think the woman and I are linked now, giraffe to giraffe, a tiny species saved from extinction by happenstance. I like to think that somewhere the house is humming its approval, that even absence can be a form of conservation.

XIII.  
I have never learned to swim. Every summer I promise myself lessons, and every autumn I console myself with the rationale that drowning is statistically rare if you stay away from water. Yet the body remembers what the mind refuses: in dreams I am always thigh-deep, the sand sucking at my feet like a debtor. A wave rears up, translucent green, and I know—dream knowledge, absolute—that once it folds over me I will never surface. I wake gasping and catalog all the water in the house: the toilet tank, the ice trays, the kettle, the human body sixty percent composed of traitor seas. I picture the cells plotting mutiny, the blood raising miniature tridents, the heart sprouting gills it cannot use. I picture the hum as sonar, bouncing off the ribs like submarine cliffs. If I could swim, would it sound different? Would the echo grow wider, softer, dissipate like gossip in deep water? Or would it amplify, turning my chest into a resonating chamber for the ocean’s minor chords? I decide, again, to stay terrestrial. On land I can pretend the floorboards are hulls, the attic a crow’s nest, the hum the creak of a ship that goes nowhere but never sinks.

XIV.  
My grandmother kept a diary for one year—1946. Each entry is exactly four lines, no more, no less. She records weather, prices, deaths, births, and what song she danced to at the canteen. Under March 3: Snow again. Eggs fourteen cents. Betty’s twins. “Don’t Fence Me In.” I found the book after she died, wedged beneath the scorched saucer she used as an ashtray. I tried to do the math: fourteen cents equals two subway rides, or one milkshake, or a single condom if you knew the druggist. I tried to imagine Betty’s twins swaddled under the same snow that fenced her in. I tried to hear the song through the crackle of a jukebox wired for war survivors, tried to feel the linoleum under rayon stockings, tried to decide whether “don’t fence me in” is plea or prophecy. The entry ends, as always, at four lines; the diary itself is only forty pages. On the last page she wrote, in a different ink, This is enough. I keep it on the nightstand and sometimes read a random page aloud, incantatory, like casting bones. The hum lowers itself to listen; the lightbulb flickers; the twins, grown and gone, somewhere in the diaspora of lineage, perhaps dance to the same song in a kitchen with granite countertops and induction stoves. Perhaps they hum the chorus while stirring quinoa, never knowing that the fence, too, was passed down, invisible but measured in four-line increments.

XV.  
I used to think midlife would feel like a summit: panoramic, breeze in the hair, a little plaque announcing Elevation 3,000 Feet, Congratulations. Instead it feels like a rest stop on the New Jersey Turnpike: the smell of diesel mixed with cinnamon rolls, fluorescent lights humming their own tune, a vending machine that sells socks and phone chargers and single Tylenol packets like relics of a future civilization. You arrive cranky and bladder-pressed, you pee, you buy coffee that tastes of wet cardboard, you squint at a map you will forget to fold correctly. You do not feel elevated; you feel interim. You Google “signs of midlife crisis” and the algorithm offers convertibles, affairs, pottery classes. You scroll past an ad for kintsugi kits and laugh so hard the coffee dribbles down your chin. In the stall someone has written LIFE IS A MOVIE AND I NEED A REFUND. You add in smaller letters: Or an intermission. You drive away. The highway unfolds like a zipper miles ahead, revealing nothing but more asphalt. Somewhere the house keeps humming, a custodian waiting to lock up after everyone has gone.

XVI.  
Once, on a plane that hit turbulence over Lake Erie, the cabin lights cut out and for ten full seconds we fell. Phones flew upward, a rain of small moons, then clattered down like metallic birds shot mid-migration. A collective gasp inverted into silence so absolute I heard the plane’s aluminum skin stretch. In that black pocket I felt no fear, only certainty: the hum had followed me into the sky, had colonized the fuselage, had become the note the engines sang as they swallowed themselves. Then the lights returned, the captain spoke of “a little chop,” the flight attendants scurried to pick up miniature pretzels from the aisle like parishioners collecting spilled communion. The man beside me asked if I was okay; I realized I was smiling. I wanted to explain that for the first time I had outrun the house, that the hum was no longer my private haunting but a communal cradle. Instead I nodded, buckled up, accepted a ginger ale. When we landed I kissed the ground, not out of gratitude but curiosity: would the tarmac taste of the lake, the sky, the cabin pressure, the hundred hearts that almost stopped? It tasted of salt and oil and something metallic, maybe blood, maybe coins. I checked my pulse—steady, traitorous—and called a taxi. That night the house sounded different, as if it had eavesdropped on my infidelity and chosen retaliation: the hum now had a vibrato, a tremor of lake water, a flirtation with altitude. I considered moving. I considered silence. I considered how every house is a plane that never takes off, every plane a house that refuses to land.

XVII.  
I keep a list of things I will never finish: the scarf I started knitting during the 2003 blackout, now a sleeve for a ghost; the Proust I bought in college because the cover matched my sheets; the letter to my father explaining why I never learned to mow the lawn in straight lines; the letter to my mother explaining that her religion of Cheerful Practicality saved me from drowning, though not from the fear of it; the letter to myself at twenty, promising it gets better, citing no sources. The list lives in the same notebook that records grocery lists and the starter’s feeding schedule, so that “call dentist” shares a page with “the body remembers for you.” Some evenings I open to a random page and add a line: The scarf is now only memory of fingers. Some evenings I write nothing, only stare at the paper until the fibers resemble topographical maps, at which point I close the notebook and open the refrigerator and stare at that too, as though the answer might be behind the pickle jar, pickled.

XVIII.  
I read, somewhere in the landfill of social media, that the universe will end in either heat death or a Big Crunch, depending on how much dark matter weighs. The article used a metaphor: if the cosmos is a dinner party, dark matter is the guest who never speaks but whose mass determines whether the table collapses. I like that—the idea that the most important entity is mute. I wonder what the universe’s hum sounds like, whether it is identical to the one under my floorboards or merely a fanfare in a different key. I wonder if, at the moment of heat death, every lost sock, every broken mug’s golden drool, every unfinished scarf will reassemble in a final blaze of reverse entropy, a kintsugi of time itself. I picture the house lifting off its foundation like a slow rocket, the hum resolving into a major chord, my grandmother stepping out of the diary in her saddle shoes, ready to dance. “Don’t fence me in,” we will all sing, as the fence rematerializes in gilded fractures, holding us together even as it lets us go.

XIX.  
And now I have run out of words, or perhaps run into them so hard they have become transparent, like glass you forget is there until the bird smacks it. I promised 2500, but promises are photocopies of photocopies. The hum continues, indifferent to word count. The house continues, though it is now mostly metaphor. The river, wider or narrower, continues. The pear trees, bulldozed, continue in the scatter of their seeds, which ride the intestines of raccoons and emerge somewhere downstream, maybe in the drainage ditch behind the Walmart, where a child will pick a small hard fruit and bite, tasting brown sunlight, tasting frost, tasting the debt we inherit and the mercy we invent. I will stop here, though the story does not. I am going outside now to stand barefoot on the boards and listen. If you press your own feet to whatever floor you have—tile, carpet, airport linoleum, the packed earth of a campsite—you might feel it too: a low steady note, neither warning nor welcome, neither beginning nor end. Listen long enough and you will sense the message beneath the message: Keep going. Keep going. Keep going.

I wanted to tell them that removing one note collapses the chord, that the ecosystem is a house of cards wearing a house of cards as a hat. Instead I Googled “how to hack Mosquito device” and learned it oscillates at 17.4 kHz, just above the upper range of human teenagers. I downloaded an app that plays the opposite waveform, inverted 180 degrees, supposedly canceling the silence. I propped my phone on the railing and let it whine all night. The cat yowled; the neighbors complained; I gained no mosquitoes back. What returned instead was the hum, louder than ever, as though it had absorbed the missing wings and learned their frequencies. I apologized to the cat, unplugged the app, accepted that some forms of repair only widen the wound.

XI.  
I once read about a Japanese practice called kintsugi: mending broken pottery with lacquer mixed with powdered gold, highlighting the cracks instead of hiding them. The philosophy is that damage is a history to be honored. I wonder what becomes of a vessel too shattered to hold liquid, too fragmented for even gold to embrace. Do you grind it to dust and scatter it in the garden? Do you press the dust into pills and swallow a memory each morning with your antidepressants? Or do you dump it in a shoebox and shove the box under the bed, where the monsters have unionized and now hold committee meetings about lighting and dental. I tried kintsugi on an old coffee mug that had witnessed every breakup and all-nighter of my twenties. The epoxy smelled like despair and refused to set; the gold powder clumped like sarcasm. The mug now leaks along its entire circumference, a golden drool that puddles on the table like a small, shameful sun. I use it anyway. Each morning I pour coffee and watch it weep, thinking: this is what becomes of healing when vanity insists on starring in the role.

XII.  
Time dilates in laundromats. The spin cycle becomes a cosmology: the drum a galaxy, the socks rogue planets, the lint trap a black hole archiving the DNA of every garment you ever loved. I once lost a sock embroidered with tiny yellow giraffes. I waited through three full cycles, convinced it would reappear, a cotton Lazarus. It never did. Weeks later I found myself at the grocery store staring at a woman wearing mismatched socks: one solid navy, one populated by miniature giraffes. I followed her down the aisle of canned beans, heart jack-hammering, rehearsing polite ways to accuse a stranger of sock theft. At the self-checkout she lifted her foot to scratch an ankle and I saw the price tag still dangling: new socks. Pure coincidence. I went home and emptied my own sock drawer like a detective overturning a suspect’s mattress. Nothing. The sock is somewhere, perhaps in the lint-trap singularity, perhaps in a parallel hamper where the colors run in reverse and all the lost left socks form a union with all the lost right Tupperware lids. I like to think the woman and I are linked now, giraffe to giraffe, a tiny species saved from extinction by happenstance. I like to think that somewhere the house is humming its approval, that even absence can be a form of conservation.

XIII.  
I have never learned to swim. Every summer I promise myself lessons, and every autumn I console myself with the rationale that drowning is statistically rare if you stay away from water. Yet the body remembers what the mind refuses: in dreams I am always thigh-deep, the sand sucking at my feet like a debtor. A wave rears up, translucent green, and I know—dream knowledge, absolute—that once it folds over me I will never surface. I wake gasping and catalog all the water in the house: the toilet tank, the ice trays, the kettle, the human body sixty percent composed of traitor seas. I picture the cells plotting mutiny, the blood raising miniature tridents, the heart sprouting gills it cannot use. I picture the hum as sonar, bouncing off the ribs like submarine cliffs. If I could swim, would it sound different? Would the echo grow wider, softer, dissipate like gossip in deep water? Or would it amplify, turning my chest into a resonating chamber for the ocean’s minor chords? I decide, again, to stay terrestrial. On land I can pretend the floorboards are hulls, the attic a crow’s nest, the hum the creak of a ship that goes nowhere but never sinks.

XIV.  
My grandmother kept a diary for one year—1946. Each entry is exactly four lines, no more, no less. She records weather, prices, deaths, births, and what song she danced to at the canteen. Under March 3: Snow again. Eggs fourteen cents. Betty’s twins. “Don’t Fence Me In.” I found the book after she died, wedged beneath the scorched saucer she used as an ashtray. I tried to do the math: fourteen cents equals two subway rides, or one milkshake, or a single condom if you knew the druggist. I tried to imagine Betty’s twins swaddled under the same snow that fenced her in. I tried to hear the song through the crackle of a jukebox wired for war survivors, tried to feel the linoleum under rayon stockings, tried to decide whether “don’t fence me in” is plea or prophecy. The entry ends, as always, at four lines; the diary itself is only forty pages. On the last page she wrote, in a different ink, This is enough. I keep it on the nightstand and sometimes read a random page aloud, incantatory, like casting bones. The hum lowers itself to listen; the lightbulb flickers; the twins, grown and gone, somewhere in the diaspora of lineage, perhaps dance to the same song in a kitchen with granite countertops and induction stoves. Perhaps they hum the chorus while stirring quinoa, never knowing that the fence, too, was passed down, invisible but measured in four-line increments.

XV.  
I used to think midlife would feel like a summit: panoramic, breeze in the hair, a little plaque announcing Elevation 3,000 Feet, Congratulations. Instead it feels like a rest stop on the New Jersey Turnpike: the smell of diesel mixed with cinnamon rolls, fluorescent lights humming their own tune, a vending machine that sells socks and phone chargers and single Tylenol packets like relics of a future civilization. You arrive cranky and bladder-pressed, you pee, you buy coffee that tastes of wet cardboard, you squint at a map you will forget to fold correctly. You do not feel elevated; you feel interim. You Google “signs of midlife crisis” and the algorithm offers convertibles, affairs, pottery classes. You scroll past an ad for kintsugi kits and laugh so hard the coffee dribbles down your chin. In the stall someone has written LIFE IS A MOVIE AND I NEED A REFUND. You add in smaller letters: Or an intermission. You drive away. The highway unfolds like a zipper miles ahead, revealing nothing but more asphalt. Somewhere the house keeps humming, a custodian waiting to lock up after everyone has gone.

XVI.  
Once, on a plane that hit turbulence over Lake Erie, the cabin lights cut out and for ten full seconds we fell. Phones flew upward, a rain of small moons, then clattered down like metallic birds shot mid-migration. A collective gasp inverted into silence so absolute I heard the plane’s aluminum skin stretch. In that black pocket I felt no fear, only certainty: the hum had followed me into the sky, had colonized the fuselage, had become the note the engines sang as they swallowed themselves. Then the lights returned, the captain spoke of “a little chop,” the flight attendants scurried to pick up miniature pretzels from the aisle like parishioners collecting spilled communion. The man beside me asked if I was okay; I realized I was smiling. I wanted to explain that for the first time I had outrun the house, that the hum was no longer my private haunting but a communal cradle. Instead I nodded, buckled up, accepted a ginger ale. When we landed I kissed the ground, not out of gratitude but curiosity: would the tarmac taste of the lake, the sky, the cabin pressure, the hundred hearts that almost stopped? It tasted of salt and oil and something metallic, maybe blood, maybe coins. I checked my pulse—steady, traitorous—and called a taxi. That night the house sounded different, as if it had eavesdropped on my infidelity and chosen retaliation: the hum now had a vibrato, a tremor of lake water, a flirtation with altitude. I considered moving. I considered silence. I considered how every house is a plane that never takes off, every plane a house that refuses to land.

XVII.  
I keep a list of things I will never finish: the scarf I started knitting during the 2003 blackout, now a sleeve for a ghost; the Proust I bought in college because the cover matched my sheets; the letter to my father explaining why I never learned to mow the lawn in straight lines; the letter to my mother explaining that her religion of Cheerful Practicality saved me from drowning, though not from the fear of it; the letter to myself at twenty, promising it gets better, citing no sources. The list lives in the same notebook that records grocery lists and the starter’s feeding schedule, so that “call dentist” shares a page with “the body remembers for you.” Some evenings I open to a random page and add a line: The scarf is now only memory of fingers. Some evenings I write nothing, only stare at the paper until the fibers resemble topographical maps, at which point I close the notebook and open the refrigerator and stare at that too, as though the answer might be behind the pickle jar, pickled.

XVIII.  
I read, somewhere in the landfill of social media, that the universe will end in either heat death or a Big Crunch, depending on how much dark matter weighs. The article used a metaphor: if the cosmos is a dinner party, dark matter is the guest who never speaks but whose mass determines whether the table collapses. I like that—the idea that the most important entity is mute. I wonder what the universe’s hum sounds like, whether it is identical to the one under my floorboards or merely a fanfare in a different key. I wonder if, at the moment of heat death, every lost sock, every broken mug’s golden drool, every unfinished scarf will reassemble in a final blaze of reverse entropy, a kintsugi of time itself. I picture the house lifting off its foundation like a slow rocket, the hum resolving into a major chord, my grandmother stepping out of the diary in her saddle shoes, ready to dance. “Don’t fence me in,” we will all sing, as the fence rematerializes in gilded fractures, holding us together even as it lets us go.

XIX.  
And now I have run out of words, or perhaps run into them so hard they have become transparent, like glass you forget is there until the bird smacks it. I promised 2500, but promises are photocopies of photocopies. The hum continues, indifferent to word count. The house continues, though it is now mostly metaphor. The river, wider or narrower, continues. The pear trees, bulldozed, continue in the scatter of their seeds, which ride the intestines of raccoons and emerge somewhere downstream, maybe in the drainage ditch behind the Walmart, where a child will pick a small hard fruit and bite, tasting brown sunlight, tasting frost, tasting the debt we inherit and the mercy we invent. I will stop here, though the story does not. I am going outside now to stand barefoot on the boards and listen. If you press your own feet to whatever floor you have—tile, carpet, airport linoleum, the packed earth of a campsite—you might feel it too: a low steady note, neither warning nor welcome, neither beginning nor end. Listen long enough and you will sense the message beneath the message: Keep going. Keep going. Keep going.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
